segments.s104-g97: سه دیگر is a noun phrase meaning “three others.” چو here is a simile particle meaning “like/as,” introducing a comparison to the following name. کشواد زرین‌کلاه is a personal name plus epithet (literally “Keshvad the Golden-Capped”); the epithet زرین‌کلاه is a compound attached to the name.
segments.s104-g98: که is a relative pronoun “who/that,” linking this clause to the preceding phrase. آمد is simple past “came.” به آمل is a prepositional phrase “to Amol.” ببرد is a present/subjunctive-form verb used here to express purpose/result (“to carry off / to take away”); classical Persian often uses a second verb like this after آمد to show intent. آن سپاه is the object “that host/army.”
segments.s104-g99: چهارم is the ordinal “the fourth.” چو again is the simile/“like.” مهراب here is a proper name (Mehrab), though the listed senses give only the common noun meanings for 'mihrab' — the poem uses it as a person. کابل names the place (Kabul). خدای in this apposition functions as an honorific/title meaning ‘lord/ruler of’ the preceding place (an archaic usage), not the religious sense.
segments.s104-g100: که is a relative pronoun “who/that,” introducing a descriptive clause. دستور شاهست is an archaic/lightly contracted copular construction: دستور is being used as a title/role (here effectively “commander” or “chief of the forces”), and شاهست (= شاه است) states relation to the king—i.e. “is the king’s commander.” و simply links the next apposition; زابل خدای repeats the same place+honorific pattern (“the lord of Zabul”).
segments.s104-g1: برفت is an archaic past-tense verb meaning 'he went/left' (third-person singular); the sentence uses the prepositional phrase از لب رود = 'from the lip/brink of the river' where لب (lit. 'lip') is used in the genitive sense 'bank' and رود = 'river'; نزد before a personal name (نزد پشنگ) means 'toward/in the presence of Pashang' (classical word order: verb then prepositional source then goal).
segments.s104-g2: زبان پر ز گفتار is a verbless descriptive phrase: زبان 'tongue/speech' + پر 'full (of)' + ز (poetic 'of/from') + گفتار 'words/speech' → 'tongue full of words'; the conjunction و links the second nominal phrase کوتاه چنگ, where کوتاه 'short/brief' modifies چنگ 'harp' (here 'harp-playing' or the instrument), giving a parallel description of the speaker's manner.
segments.s104-g3: بدو گفت is the classical word order 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him/unto him' + گفت = 'he said'); کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee; نامبردار شاه is the epithet 'renowned/famed king' that follows the vocative.
segments.s104-g4: This clause is an impersonal-styled copular construction: ترا (here 'to you' / dative) + بود (past 'was') + [infinitive phrase] + گناه (predicate noun 'sin'). ازین جنگ جستن is an infinitive phrase 'to seek this war' functioning as the subject/thing judged wrong; thus the whole means 'It was a sin for you to seek this war.' Note the orthographic split ترا → ت + را in the segment list; treat them together as the dative 'for you'.
segments.s104-g5: یکی here functions as a discourse enumerator 'one (point/reason)'; آنکه is the relative 'he who' (introducing the clause); پیمان شکستن is an infinitival noun phrase 'to break a covenant/pledge'; ز شاه uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from/with' to indicate 'with the king' (i.e. breaking covenant with the king).
segments.s104-g6: بزرگان پیشین ('the great ones/elders of old') is the subject; ندیدند is the past tense 'they did not see' (negative formed here by the prefix ن- on the verb), and راه ('way') is the object — together 'the old great men did not see such a way,' i.e. they had no precedent for it. Word order is Subject + Verb + Object, with ندیدند negating 'see.'
segments.s104-g7: This clause begins the correlative negation with نه ... نه. نه ... جهان پاک شد = 'the world did not become/was not made pure' and از تخم ایرج ('from the seed/offspring of Iraj') specifies what the world was not freed of. شد is the past copular/auxiliary 'became/was made.'
segments.s104-g8: The second half of the correlative negation: نه introduces the parallel clause. زهر گزاینده تریاک شد reads 'opium did not become a گزاینده (here 'biter'/'stinging') poison' — i.e. 'opium did not turn into a biting/venomous poison.' Again شد is the past 'became' and the sequence is noun + modifier + predicate.
segments.s104-g89: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb 'now'. از گذشته is a prepositional phrase 'from the past'. مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'; here it governs یاد, so یاد (literally 'remembrance') functions as the verbalized object meaning 'remember/recall'. هیچ is an intensifying negative 'nothing/at all' modifying یاد. The overall order is emphatic/poetic but means 'Now, do not at all recall the past.'
segments.s104-g90: سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. آشتی is the noun 'reconciliation/peace' as the goal. یاز is an imperative/hortative verb in this poetic usage meaning 'turn/open toward' or 'seek' (an older/poetic verb form). با marks accompaniment 'with', and کیقباد is the proper name 'Kay Qobad'. Together this gives the command 'turn toward reconciliation with Kay Qobad.'
segments.s104-g91: گرت is an archaic/poetic conditional 'if (it be that)' functioning like 'اگر'. دیگر modifies the following noun phrase as 'another' or 'more'. آید is the present/optative verb 'comes' (used in the protasis). یکی آرزوی is a noun phrase 'a certain/a single wish' placed after the verb by poetic inversion: 'if another a wish comes' = 'if another wish comes.'
segments.s104-g92: به گرد is a locative phrase 'around' or 'about', اندر is an older preposition 'in/within' used here to strengthen the sense of encirclement, and آید is the verb 'comes'. سپه is the subject 'army/host' and چارسوی is an adverbial 'from the four directions' (literally 'four-sides'). Word order is poetic: the verb phrase 'be-gard andar ayad' means 'comes encircling' and applies to 'sepeh chārsū' = 'an army from all sides.'
segments.s104-g93: به یک دست is an idiomatic phrase 'on one hand / on one side' (دست used metaphorically as 'side'). رستم is the proper name. که introduces a relative clause describing him. تابنده is a participial adjective 'shining' modifying هور (an epithet meaning 'splendor' or 'light'); together 'تابنده هور' is a laudatory epithet applied to Rostam. The noun phrase order is poetic but straightforward: 'on one side, Rostam, the shining Hoor...'.
segments.s104-g94: گه is an adverb 'on the occasion/when' (here introducing the time of battle). رزم 'battle' names the occasion. با او 'with/against him' marks the adversary relation. نتابد is negative present/perfect sense from تابیدن here meaning 'does not endure/stand' (so 'cannot withstand'). به زور 'by force' modifies نتابد. The clause means 'when in battle with him, no strength/endurance stands against him.'
segments.s104-g95: به روی دگر is the parallel idiom 'on the other face/side' = 'on the other hand'. قارن is the proper name. رزم‌زن is an epithet 'warrior / battle-striker' in apposition to the name. The word order lists the contrasting side and then the name with its epithet.
segments.s104-g96: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' linking to قارن. چشمش is 'his eye' (possessive clitic -ش). ندیده‌ست is the perfect negative 'has not seen' (ندیدن + auxiliary ست). هرگز is the emphatic negative 'ever/never' reinforcing ندیده‌ست. شکن 'breaker' is the object: 'his eye has never seen a breaker' i.e. he has never encountered anyone who can break/defeat him. The negation is standard Persian perfect-negative construction, with هرگز strengthening the negation.
segments.s104-g33: تو دانی که = 'you know that': تو is the 2nd-person singular pronoun, دانی is the present indicative 2sg of دان(ستن) 'to know', and که introduces a content clause. شاهی دل و چنگ من is the subject phrase of that clause: شاهی (kingship/royal quality) modifies دل و چنگ (heart and harp), followed by the possessive من 'my'.
segments.s104-g34: به جنگ اندرون زور و آهنگ من = 'in/into battle, within my strength and intent': به + جنگ marks location/goal ('in/into battle'), اندرون is an adverbial/locative 'within/inside' modifying جنگ, and زور و آهنگ من are two coordinated nouns ('strength and intention/aim') with the possessive من attached at the end to mean 'my'. آهنگ here means 'intention/aim' rather than melody.
segments.s104-g35: به دست وی اندر یکی پشه‌ام = 'in his hand I am a single gnat': به دست is a fixed locative phrase 'in/at the hand (power) of', وی is the 3rd-person pronoun (here functioning possessively 'his'), اندر is another locative particle 'within/in', and یکی پشه‌ام literally 'I am one gnat' (پشه with the 1sg suffix -ام = 'I am [a] gnat' or 'I am like a gnat').
segments.s104-g36: وزان آفرینش پر اندیشه‌ام = 'and from that creation/making I am full of thought': وزان is a conjunctive/adversative 'and/from that', آفرینش here means 'creation/that making' (not praise), پر is the preposed adjective 'full (of)', and اندیشه‌ام = 'my thought' or 'I am full of thought' (اندیشه 'thought' with the 1sg suffix -ام). Overall it expresses that the speaker is filled with thought as a result of that event.
segments.s104-g37: یکی پیلتن دیدم و شیرچنگ = 'once/one (time) at Piltan I saw (it), and Shirchang': یکی can function adverbially 'once' or 'one', پیلتن is a place/name (object or locative context supplied by the narrative), and دیدم is 1st-person singular past 'I saw'. The final و شیرچنگ links an additional object/name seen (Shirchang or 'lion-claw'). Word order places the time/quantity and place before the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s104-g38: نه هوش و نه دانش نه رای و درنگ = 'neither wit nor learning, nor counsel nor hesitation': نه ... و نه ... is the standard parallel negative construction 'neither ... nor ...'. The four nouns are listed as lacking qualities: هوش (intellect/wit), دانش (knowledge/learning), رای (here 'counsel/plan'), and درنگ (delay/hestitation or deliberation).
segments.s104-g39: عنان را سپرده بران پیل مست = 'having entrusted the reins to that drunken elephant': عنان را uses را as the definite/object marker on عنان 'the reins', سپرده is the past participle/past form 'entrusted' (here indicating a completed action), and بران پیل مست means 'to/upon that elephant (پیل) which is مست (drunk or intoxicated, possibly figuratively). The phrase is a compact past participial clause meaning 'he had entrusted the reins to that drunken elephant.'
segments.s104-g40: یکی گرزه گاو‌پیکر بدست = 'one mace, bull-shaped, in hand': یکی 'one/a certain', گرزه 'mace/cudgel' is the noun, گاو‌پیکر is an adjectival compound 'bull-formed/bull-shaped' modifying گرزه, and بدست is a locative/adverbial 'in the hand' (i.e. was held in hand). The whole phrase describes a single bull-shaped mace held in hand.
segments.s104-g25: چنان is an adverb meaning “so/thus” modifying برگرفتم. برگرفتم is past tense first person singular (بر + گرفتم) = “I took up.” ز is the poetic form of از (“from”), so ز زین خدنگ means “from the saddle (زین) the dagger (خدنگ).” Word order is adverb + verb + preposition + object.
segments.s104-g26: که introduces the subordinate/reported clause (“that / as”), گفتی here is intended as the 2nd-person past “you said” (the provided sense list does not include this verbal sense, so marked -1). ندارم is the negative present first person (“I do not have”); به introduces the beneficiary/measure sense “for / even for”; یک پشه سنگ literally “a stone for one gnat” (an idiom for something extremely small or meagre). The clause reports speech or a proverb quoted by the addressee.
segments.s104-g27: کمربند (girdle/belt) is the subject; بگسست is past tense ‘broke’ (ب- prefix + گسست). و links the next noun phrase; بند قبای = “the fastening/band of the robe” (بند = band/fastening, قبای = robe). The verb governing the whole clause is بگسست.
segments.s104-g28: ز چنگش = “from his grip” (ز = from; چنگ + ش = grip/claw + possessive “his”). فتادم is first person past “I fell” (or “it befell me”). نگون is an adjective/adverb “overthrown / downcast,” modifying the fall. زیر پای = “under the foot.” The sequence omits a copula; the clauses describe the manner/result of the fall.
segments.s104-g29: بدان is the imperative ‘know this’ formed from the prefix ب- + دان (to know); here it addresses the listener. زور (strength/might) is the theme/topic. هرگز نباشد is a negative modal/present-subjunctive phrase meaning “will never be / there will never be”; placing هرگز before the verb intensifies the negation. هژبر is a proper name (the person being spoken about).
segments.s104-g30: دو پایش = “his two feet” (دو = two; پای + ش = foot + his). به خاک اندر is an archaic/prepositional phrase meaning “in/into the earth” (اندر = in/within). و links the second image; سر به ابر = “(his) head to the cloud(s)” (literally ‘head to cloud’). The copula is omitted—these two phrases illustrate the impossible posture implied in the previous clause.
segments.s104-g31: سواران جنگی = “war-horsemen / mounted warriors” (سواران plural + جنگی ‘warlike’). همه emphasizes totality “all.” همگروه is an epithet meaning ‘fellow-members / comrades’ and further qualifies the group; word order places modifiers before the verb that follows.
segments.s104-g32: کشیدندم is past tense 3rd-person plural کشیدند ‘they pulled/dragged’ with the enclitic object -م “me” (they dragged me). از پیش آن لخت کوه is a prepositional phrase “from before that bare mountain” (از پیش = from/in front of; آن = that; لخت کوه = bare mountain). The clause describes the action on the speaker.
segments.s104-g41: همانا که is an emphatic introductory phrase: همانا (“verily”) + the complementizer/relativizer که introducing the following clause. کوپال (here treated as a named weapon) is the head noun and سیصدهزار is a numeric qualifier placed after it for poetic effect (ʻthree hundred thousandʼ modifying/qualifying کوپال).
segments.s104-g42: زدندش is the past plural verb زدند (“they struck”) with the enclitic object pronoun -ش (“him”), so ‘they struck him’. بران is the prepositional/locative particle بر + آن contracted in poetry meaning ‘upon that’ or ‘on that’. تارک ترگ‌دار is a two-word adjectival/nominal phrase modifying the place/part struck: تارک (here an adjectival/nominal element) + ترگ‌دار (‘cracked, with a crack’); the second word is a descriptive compound ending the clause.
segments.s104-g43: تو گفتی که is reported speech: تو گفتی (‘you said’) + که introducing the reported content. از marks material or source (‘from’), آهنش is آهن + possessive -ش (‘its/his iron’), and کرده‌اند is the compound perfect/past result (کرده‌اند, ‘they have made/done’), so the clause means ‘you said that they had made it from its iron’. Note: گفتی is the past tense 2nd-singular verb (you said).
segments.s104-g44: ز سنگ و ز رویش are two coordinated prepositional phrases (ز = poetic form of از, ‘from’) listing materials or sources: from stone and from its surface (روی + possessive -ش). برآورده‌اند is a perfect/result verb (برآورده‌اند) meaning ‘they have brought forth/produced’ (literally ‘have brought up’); it continues the same reported action introduced by کرده‌اند.
segments.s104-g45: The pair چه … و چه introduces a correlative listing ‘whether … or …’. چه is functioning as the correlative particle (‘whether/ be it’). دریاش is دریا + possessive -ش (‘his/its sea’), and پیش (‘before/in front’) is an adverbial indicating position. ببر is a noun (‘tiger’) following the second چه; بیان here is part of the noun phrase but its exact meaning is context-sensitive and ambiguous (could be a name/place or an attributive word), so it remains a noun element in apposition.
segments.s104-g46: Again the correlative structure چه … و چه lists two more threatening animals. درنده is an adjective ‘ferocious/predatory’ modifying شیر (‘lion’). The second چه introduces پیل ژیان (‘a living elephant’ or ‘an elephant alive’), where ژیان (‘life’) qualifies پیل; the whole phrase enumerates possible foes.
segments.s104-g47: همی‌تاخت is an imperfective/past continuous form (poetic use of همی‑ + past stem) meaning ‘he was charging’ or ‘kept charging’. یکسان (‘alike, equally’) qualifies the manner of the charge. چو روز شکار is a simile: چو = ‘as/like’, روز شکار = ‘a hunting day’ (literal ‘day of the hunt’), so ‘he rushed on in the same way as on a hunting day.’
segments.s104-g48: به بازی is an adverbial phrase ‘as a play/for sport’ (به marking the comparative manner). همی‌آمدش is the same poetic imperfective with enclitic -ش (‘it came upon him’ or ‘it kept coming to him’) where the clitic refers back to the person/thing affected. کارزار is the noun ‘battle/field of battle’ and appears at the clause end (verb before subject/object is common in poetry); the sequence means ‘the battle came upon him as if it were a game.’
segments.s104-g17: همی is a classical progressive particle placed before a past-tense verb to show repeated or continuous action; تاخت is the past of تاختن (to rush/gallop). اندر + noun phrase (فراز و نشیب) is a prepositional phrase meaning “in/on the ups and downs” (فراز = heights/top, نشیب = descent), joined by و. Word order: particle + verb, then the locative phrase.
segments.s104-g18: همی again marks repeated action; زد is the simple past of زدن (to strike). The preposition به here means instrument/means ("by/with"), so به گرز = "with the mace"; the repeated به before تیغ emphasizes the separate instruments. رکیب at the end names the person targeted ("rival/opponent"). The clause lists instruments/targets in a paratactic series.
segments.s104-g19: ز is the poetic preposition meaning “from/by,” introducing the cause (ز گرزش = “from/by his blow”). هوا is the grammatical subject, شد is the past of شدن (to become), and پر از + noun expresses fullness (هوا شد پر از چاک چاک = "the air became full of tearing/rending"). چاک چاک is a doubl‑form used for emphasis: repeated gash/tear.
segments.s104-g20: نیرزید is an archaic past form meaning here “did not merit / was not reduced” (negative sense), followed by the possessor + noun جانم (“my life” — جان + possessive -م written separately in the segmentation). The prepositional phrase به یک مشت خاک gives the measure/endpoint (“to a single handful of dust”). The overall sense is a poetic negative result: the speaker’s life was not left even as a handful of dust.
segments.s104-g21: Subject comes first (همه لشکر ما = “our whole army/host”), then the verbal phrase به هم بردرید. به هم here expresses reciprocal action/togetherness (“one another”/“among themselves”), and بردرید is the past tense meaning “tore apart” or “cut to pieces.” The clause describes the army’s mutual destruction/scattering.
segments.s104-g22: کس ... ندید is a negative existential pattern meaning “no one saw”; اندر جهان is an adverbial phrase “in the world.” این شگفتی is the direct object phrase “this wonder/marvel.” Word order: topical adverbial (اندر جهان) can appear between subject and object, and ندید is the past tense of دیدن.
segments.s104-g23: درفش is the noun “standard/banner.” The sequence درفش مرا دید parses as object + possessive/definite marking: here م (the possessive suffix written separately) + را (the definite/object marker) marks the banner as the direct object (“my banner/standard”). دید is the past of دیدن (saw). بر یک کران is a locative phrase (“on one edge/flank”) with بر + numeral + noun.
segments.s104-g24: به زین = “to/onto the saddle” (به + زین). اندر آورد is a compound verbal sequence meaning “brought in/put into” (اندر here reinforces interior placement). گرز گران is the direct object (گرز = mace, گران = heavy), adjective after noun in classical Persian describing weight/quality.
segments.s104-g9: Literary clause with subject-first order: 'یکی کم شود' (literally 'one becomes few') uses کم + شود = 'is diminished/one is lost'. دیگری آید is a simple present/imperf. 'دیگر آید' = 'another comes (in)'. The prepositional phrase 'به جای' ('in place') follows the verb, so the whole segment reads 'if one is lost, another comes in his place.'
segments.s104-g10: جهان را is topicalized with the object marker را. نماند(َ) here is an older/poetic negative of ماندن and functions as 'did not remain/was not left.' بی‌کدخدای is a compound with the privative prefix بی- 'without' + کدخدا 'village-chief/leader', so the clause means 'the world was not left without a chief.'
segments.s104-g11: Simple narrative clause: proper name + آمد = 'Qobad came.' The coordination 'و' links the next action: 'تاج بر سر نهاد' is a verb phrase with object first (تاج) then prepositional phrase بر سر 'upon the head' and نهاده (نهاد) as the perfect/past participle of نهادن 'to place', here past 'placed/put on', so 'he placed the crown on (his) head.'
segments.s104-g12: The idiom به کینه = 'out of hatred/spite' uses به + noun to indicate motive. یکی نو = 'a new one' or 'a new [thing/episode]' and در اندر گشاد literally 'opened a door within' (در = 'door' here, اندر = 'inside/within', گشاد = 'opened' or 'wide'), a poetic way to say 'he opened a new breach/rupture inside (the realm/community)'. Note the elliptical, poetic order typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s104-g13: Compound verbal phrase: سواری (a rider/horseman) is the subject, پدید آمد is a literary two-part verb meaning 'appeared/manifested' (پدید + آمد = 'came into view'). از تخم سام uses تخم 'seed/line' with از to indicate descent: 'from the seed/line of Sam' (i.e., descended from Sam).
segments.s104-g14: Relative clause introduced by که ('who/whose'): دستانش here literally 'his hands' but in heroic poetry 'دست' is used metonymically for 'deeds/feats'; نهاده‌ست is the contracted perfect 'نهاده است' = 'has placed/given', and نام = 'name'. The clause therefore means 'whose deeds have given him the name Rostam.'
segments.s104-g15: Simile introduced by بسان ('like/as'): بیامد is a poetic past 'he came/arrived', followed by the simile object 'نهنگ دژم' = 'a grim/sullen whale' (a large, fearsome image). Adjective دژم ('grim, sullen') modifies نهنگ.
segments.s104-g16: Relative/explanatory clause: که + گفتی is a colloquial/poetic formula here meaning 'one would say' or 'as if to say' (گفتی is used idiomatically). زمین را is the definite object 'the earth/ground'. بسوزد is subjunctive/optative meaning 'would burn' (here hypothetical/pictorial), and به دم 'with/by the breath' expresses the instrument. The clause pictures his breath as so terrible that 'one might say it could burn the earth.'
segments.s104-g65: نگر is an imperative 'look!/see!'. تا چه مایه is an idiomatic measure phrase meaning 'to what extent/how much' (تا + interrogative چه + مایه 'source/amount'). ستام here is the noun 'praise' and بزر is 'the great (one)'/‘great’ used postpositively in this poetic ordering, so مایه ستام بزر = 'the source/extent of the great one's praise'.
segments.s104-g66: هم 'also/even' qualifies what follows; از marks source 'from/of'. ترگ زرین = 'golden rag/garment' (ترگ = rag/cloth, زرین = golden), and و زرین سپر = 'and (a) golden shield' (زرین before سپر = adjective + noun). The whole phrase supplies the material/source referred to by مایه.
segments.s104-g67: همان is a demonstrative 'the same' pointing back; تازی modifies اسپان to mean 'Arabian horses/steeds' (poetic adjective + plural noun). زرین لگام is 'golden bridle' (زرین = golden, لگام = bridle/reins). The phrase lists a spoil/equipment item, with typical poetic inversions.
segments.s104-g68: Again همان 'the same'; تیغ هندی = 'Indian sword' (تیغ = sword, هندی = Indian/Indian-style). به زرین نیام uses به as a preposition indicating enclosure/attachement ('in/with'), زرین = 'golden' and نیام = 'scabbard/sheath', so 'in a golden scabbard'.
segments.s104-g69: ازین بیشتر is a compound adverbial 'besides this / moreover' (از + این written here as ازین, with ین representing the orthographic element). نامداران گرد = 'the renowned ones around/about' (نامداران = famed men, گرد = 'around' or 'gathered about').
segments.s104-g70: قباد is the proper name subject. اندر آمد = 'came in/entered among' (اندر = in/among, آمد = came). به خواری ببرد is a verbal phrase 'carried/took (him) away in humiliation' (به خواری = 'into humiliation', ببرد here = 'take/carry off' in the narrative sense).
segments.s104-g71: چو and (second) چون are the poetic 'like/as' used to compare; the two names کلباد and بارمان are listed in parallel joined by و. دلیر 'brave' qualifies (especially) بارمان but by listing may color both names; word order and repetition are stylistic.
segments.s104-g72: که is the relative 'who/which'; بودی is the past copula 'were' (narrative past). شکارش is شکار + enclitic -ش 'his/their quarry/hunt' (the possessive clitic attached to شکار), همه = 'all', نره شیر = 'male lions' (نره = male, شیر = lion). The clause states that their prey consisted entirely of male lions.
segments.s104-g57: تو دانی is a simple present, 2nd-person singular ('you know') with تو (subject) + دانی (present). که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). دیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'seeing'. نه before a comparative sense (چون) negates the comparison: 'seeing is not like ...'. آگهیست is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person copula (-ست) with آگهی 'awareness/knowing' as predicate: 'is awareness'.
segments.s104-g58: میان is a prepositional/adverbial 'between' heading a phrase; شنیدن is a gerund/masdar 'hearing' (the counterpart to دیدن). همیشه is an adverb 'always' modifying the predicate. تهیست is the predicate 'is empty/devoid' (poetic copula -ست again): literally 'between (them) hearing is always empty', i.e. there is a gap/void between seeing and hearing.
segments.s104-g59: گلستان 'rose-garden' is the head noun. که here functions as a relative/conditional 'that/which/if'. امروز is the temporal adverb 'today'. باشد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive copula ('may be / is'); in poetry it can express possibility. ببار is used poetically here (originally 'to pour' / imperative in the lexicon) and is best read as an intransitive poetic verb 'to bear/come into bloom' (i.e. 'that is in bloom/that bears flowers').
segments.s104-g60: تو is the addressee 'you' (subject/indirect object here). فردا 'tomorrow' is a temporal adverb. چنی is a demonstrative/adjective 'such'. گل 'flower' is the noun modified by چنی. نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive/optative ('will not come / will not appear'); here it means 'will not be produced/appear'. بکار is the idiomatic phrase 'به کار آمدن' (here roughly 'come into use / come to be useful/available'), so نیاید بکار = 'will not come into being / will not be available'.
segments.s104-g61: از امروز is a temporal prepositional phrase 'from today / starting today'. کاری is a noun here 'a deed/task/work' (lexical entry also lists planting senses, but context gives general 'work'). بفردا = 'to/till tomorrow' (بـ + فردا). ممان is an older/poetic negative imperative meaning 'do not withhold / do not delay' (i.e. 'do not postpone'). The verb comes at the end (typical Persian SOV ordering with the imperative-final sense).
segments.s104-g62: که داند is a rhetorical clause 'who knows' (که introduces indirect question or exclamation). The following که introduces the embedded content 'that/what': فردا چه گردد زمان؟ literally 'tomorrow what will become/time?' — گردد is subjunctive/third-person 'become / happen' and زمان 'time' here is the impersonal subject ('who knows what time will bring / what will happen tomorrow'). The clause is an interrogative rhetorical question linked to the admonition not to postpone.
segments.s104-g63: ترا here is the old-fashioned object form 'to/for you' (written in two pieces: ت + را). جنگ ایران 'the war of Iran' is the subject. چو is a conjunction 'when / as'. بازی نمود is a verbal phrase where بازی is 'play/sport' and نمود is past tense/past-stem of نشاندن/نمودن 'to show/make' — together 'made (a) play of', i.e. 'toyed with / treated as a game'. Word order is Poetic (object pronoun enclitic form split off).
segments.s104-g64: ز بازی 'from/through that play' (ز = 'from/because of'). سپه را marks 'the host/army' with the object marker را. درازی 'length/size' is the thing that was increased; فزود is a past-tense verb 'increased / added'. The verb follows its object phrases (poetic SOV): literally 'from the play it added length to the army' = 'the skirmish increased the army's numbers'.
segments.s104-g81: گر is the conditional 'if'. از من here precedes the noun phrase and carries the sense 'because of / through me' (cause agent). سر نامور is a noun + adjective ('head, famous'); گشته‌شد is an older poetic/periphrastic past/passive meaning 'became' or 'was made' (گشته + شد), so the clause literally reads 'if by me a head became renowned.'
segments.s104-g82: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the specific event. اغریرث is a proper name; پرخرد is an epithet modifying it ('very wise'); کشته‌شد is the past passive ('was killed' / 'was slain') formed with the passive participle + شد, a standard poetic past-passive construction.
segments.s104-g83: This is a coordination: جوانی بد = 'bad youth' and نیکی روزگار = 'the goodness/kindness of (one's) days/fate'. Note the noun‑adjective and noun‑noun patterns: نیکی modifies روزگار (goodness of fate), while بد qualifies جوانی (the bad side of youth).
segments.s104-g84: امروز را topicalizes/objects 'today' with the object marker را. دی here is the substantive 'the former / past' (i.e. bygone days). گرفتم is 1st‑person past 'I took', and شمار is a noun 'count/tally' — together گرفتم شمار is the idiom 'I took an account / I counted up'. Word order is slightly fronted for emphasis (object/topic before verb).
segments.s104-g85: که again introduces the clause. پیش آمدندم is آمدند ('they came') + the enclitic ـم marking 'to me' (i.e. 'they came before/to me'); پیش here is adverbial 'before/in front of / up to'. همان is the demonstrative 'those very/the same', and سرکشان is the subject ('the headstrong/rebellious ones' in sense; here it names the group that came).
segments.s104-g86: پس پشت is an adverbial cluster meaning 'behind/in the rear'; هر یک = 'each one'. درفشی کشان is a participial phrase: درفشی = 'standard/banner (bearer)' and کشان is the present participle/agentive form 'drawing/dragging' (‑شان forms a continuous participle here), so the phrase describes each man as 'dragging/trailing a standard behind him.'
segments.s104-g87: بسی here functions adverbially 'much' / 'greatly'. یاد دادندم is the idiom 'they gave me memory' = 'they reminded me' (دادندم = 'they gave me'). از روزگار marks the source/topic of the reminder ('about the times/fate'). The whole clause means 'they often reminded me of the ways of fate.'
segments.s104-g88: از پس is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from/afterwards / of later times'. من دوان uses the active/participial form دوان ('running, fleeing') with the personal pronoun 'I' as the subject, so 'I running/fled'. زار و خوار are two adjectival predicates 'wretched and despised', describing the subject. The clause therefore says the speaker was in flight, pitiable and dishonored.
segments.s104-g49: چنوگر is a conditional adverb ('if such / if so') introducing a hypothetical. بدی here is a noun meaning 'harm' or 'misdeed' and سام را is the direct object marked by the enclitic را. دستبرد is a compound noun 'seizure/encroachment' that completes what happened to Sam.
segments.s104-g50: به ترکان is a prepositional phrase 'to/against the Turks.' نماندی is the past form of ماندن used in a conditional sense 'would not have been left/ remained.' سرافراز is a predicative adjective 'triumphant/lofty' and گرد is a poetic/verbal element (from گردیدن) forming the verbal predicate 'became/was' in this older style.
segments.s104-g51: جز از is the idiom 'except for / apart from.' آشتی is the noun 'reconciliation' and جستنت is the infinitive/gerund 'seeking' with the archaic enclitic -ت referring to the addressee (roughly 'your seeking'). رای نیست is an existential negative 'there is no counsel/plan.'
segments.s104-g52: که here introduces a causal clause 'for / because.' با او = 'with him.' سپاه is 'army/host,' and the attached ت is the 2nd-person enclitic marking possession ('your'); را is the definite/object/topic marker. پای نیست is a predicative phrase 'has no foot/footing' meaning 'has no firm footing/stance.'
segments.s104-g53: زمینی here denotes 'a (piece of) land / the earth' and is followed by کجا (literally 'where'); in poetic word order this yields 'a certain land' or 'a tract of earth.' آفریدون is the subject (proper name) and گرد is the poetic verbal element (from گردیدن) used here in the sense of 'made/caused/created.'
segments.s104-g54: بدانگه is an adverbial 'thereupon / at that time.' به marks the beneficiary/recipient 'to,' تور is the proper name Tūr, دلاور is an epithet 'valiant/heroic,' and سپرد is the past tense verb 'entrusted / handed over.' The whole phrase states that Afridun entrusted that land to Tur the valiant.
segments.s104-g55: به من is the indirect object 'to me.' داده بودند is pluperfect ('they had given'), و is the conjunction 'and,' بخشیده is a past participle 'forgiven,' and راست functions as an adverb/adjectival intensifier 'true/indeed/just so' — here reinforcing that the matter was genuinely forgiven.
segments.s104-g56: ترا is the archaic 2nd-person enclitic 'you' (object/target). کین پیشین is the noun phrase 'former vengeance / past grudge.' نبایست is the negative of obligation 'ought not / must not' and خواست here names the action 'to seek / to desire' (so نبایست خواست = 'you ought not to seek'). The clause admonishes the addressee not to pursue revenge for past wrongs.
segments.s104-g73: This clause is an emphatic, somewhat elliptical clause. Order: topic 'خزروان' (kings/royalty) → interrogative/adverb 'کجا' (where/how) → agent 'زال' → past verb 'بشکست' (broke/shattered). 'بشکست' is the simple past (3rd singular) of شکستَن with the archaic poetic prefix بـ; it is transitive here, taking 'خرد' as its object.
segments.s104-g74: 'نمودش' is نمود (past 'showed/made/did') plus the clitic object ـش referring back to the object/person in the previous clause; in context it functions as 'he inflicted/ dealt (it) to him.' The preposition 'به' here marks instrument/means ('with' / 'by'), so 'به گرز گران دستبرد' = 'by/with a heavy mace (گرز گران) a violent assault (دستبرد)'. 'دستبرد' is used in the sense of a forceful attack/seizure rather than mere theft.
segments.s104-g75: A nominal phrase listing a name/title plus epithets in apposition: 'شماساس' is used as a proper name/title, followed by compound epithets 'کین‌توز' (lit. 'hatred‑bearer' / 'hater') and 'لشکر‌پناه' (lit. 'army‑refuge', i.e. 'refuge/protector of the host'). All elements are in apposition describing the same person.
segments.s104-g76: Relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/whom/that') modifying the preceding name. 'قارن' is the subject (a proper name here), 'بکشتش' is the past transitive 'he killed him' (بـ‑کشت + clitic ـش = 'killed him'), and 'به آوردگاه' gives the location ('to/into the battlefield' — here 'on/at the field of battle'). Word order is subject (قارن) → verb+object (بکشتش) → locative phrase (به آوردگاه).
segments.s104-g77: 'جزین' is a particle 'aside from/except these' introducing a contrast with the named men. 'نامدران' is plural 'the famed/renowned ones'. 'کین' here is used attributively (sense 'vengeance/hatred') forming a dramatic compound with the numeral that follows; together with 'صدهزار' it points to the sheer number involved (literally 'a hundred thousand').
segments.s104-g78: 'فزون' is an adverb/adjective 'more / additional' placed before the passive/participial 'کشته' (killed/slain). 'آمد' (came) is used impersonally/poetically to report occurrence ('there came to pass / there were'). 'گه کارزار' is a locative ('in the place of battle' = 'on the battlefield'). Together: 'a hundred thousand more were killed on the battlefield.'
segments.s104-g79: 'بتر' is the comparative 'worse'. The phrase compares what follows to 'این همه' (here 'all this'); thus 'بتر زین همه' = 'worse than all these (things)'. 'نام و ننگ' is a paired nominal phrase 'name and disgrace' (here: 'fame and shame' / 'reputation and dishonor'), and 'شکست' is 'defeat' — the whole clause predicatively says that the reputation/shame of defeat was worse than the prior matters.
segments.s104-g80: 'شکستی' (a defeat) is followed by a relative clause 'که هرگز نشایدش بست'. 'که' = 'that/which'; 'هرگز' = 'never'; 'نشایدش' is نشاید (it is not fitting/it should not be) plus the clitic ـش referring back to 'شکستی' — so 'it is never fitting for it'; 'بست' here is literal 'to bind/close', used metaphorically as 'to bind up / to repair / to cover over' the defeat. The sense: 'a defeat that could never be bound up / remedied / covered.'

sentences.s104-s49: Three others, like Keshvad the Golden-Capped, who came to Amol to carry off that host.
sentences.s104-s50: The fourth, like Mehrab, lord of Kabul — who is the (king’s) commander — and the lord of Zabul.
sentences.s104-s1: He went from the river's edge to Pashang, his tongue full of speech and his harp-playing brief.
sentences.s104-s2: He said to him, 'O famed king, it was a sin for you to seek this war.'
sentences.s104-s3: One (reason) is this: he who breaks a covenant with the king — the great men of old have not seen (such a) way.
sentences.s104-s4: Neither was the world purified of the seed of Iraj, nor did opium become a biting poison.
sentences.s104-s45: Now do not recall anything of the past; turn toward reconciliation with Kay Qobad.
sentences.s104-s46: If another desire should come, let an army come about from the four directions.
sentences.s104-s47: On one hand is Rustam, the shining Hoor; when he goes to battle, no strength can withstand him.
sentences.s104-s48: On the other hand is Qaran the warrior, whose eye has never seen a breaker.
sentences.s104-s17: You know that the kingship of my heart and my harp is engaged in battle, within my strength and my purpose.
sentences.s104-s18: In his hand I am but a single gnat, and from that making I am full of thought.
sentences.s104-s19: Once I saw at Piltan a Shirchang (lion-claw), with neither wit nor learning, nor counsel nor deliberation.
sentences.s104-s20: He had entrusted the reins to that drunken elephant; a single bull-shaped mace was in hand.
sentences.s104-s13: I took up the dagger from the saddle in such a way that — as you said — “I don't have even a stone for a single gnat.”
sentences.s104-s14: The girdle broke and the robe's fastening; I fell from his grasp, overturned beneath his foot.
sentences.s104-s15: Know this: Hojabr will never have that strength — his two feet in the earth and his head in the clouds.
sentences.s104-s16: All the war-horsemen, comrades together, dragged me away from before that bare mountain.
sentences.s104-s21: Truly, they struck him with the Kupāl — three hundred thousand — upon that cracked tark.
sentences.s104-s22: You said that they had made it from its iron, and from stone and from its surface they had brought (it) forth.
sentences.s104-s23: Whether his sea was before him or a tiger of Bayān, whether a ferocious lion or a living elephant.
sentences.s104-s24: He charged alike as on a hunting day; the battle came upon him as if it were a game.
sentences.s104-s9: He kept charging over the heights and descents and kept striking with his mace and with his sword and at his rival.
sentences.s104-s10: From his mace’s blow the air became full of tearing (rends); my life did not remain to a single handful of dust.
sentences.s104-s11: Our entire host tore itself apart; no one in the world had seen such a marvel.
sentences.s104-s12: He saw my standard on one flank; he placed a heavy mace into the saddle.
sentences.s104-s5: One is lost and another comes in his place; the world did not remain without a chief.
sentences.s104-s6: Qobad came and set the crown upon his head; out of hatred he opened a new breach within.
sentences.s104-s7: A horseman appeared from the seed (line) of Sam; his deeds have set the name Rostam.
sentences.s104-s8: He came like a grim whale — one might say his breath could burn the earth.
sentences.s104-s33: See how great a source of praise it is — even from a golden rag and a golden shield.
sentences.s104-s34: The same Arabian steeds with golden bridles, the same Indian blade in a golden scabbard.
sentences.s104-s35: Moreover, more renowned men gathered around; Qobad came among them and carried him off in humiliation.
sentences.s104-s36: Like Kalbad and like the brave Barman — who were his quarry, all male lions.
sentences.s104-s29: You know that seeing is not the same as awareness; there is always a gap between seeing and hearing.
sentences.s104-s30: A rose-garden that blooms today may not yield such a flower for you tomorrow.
sentences.s104-s31: Do not put off today's work until tomorrow—who knows what will happen tomorrow?
sentences.s104-s32: When the war with Iran toyed with you, that sport increased the army's numbers.
sentences.s104-s41: If, because of me, a head became renowned — it was when Aghrirath, very wise, was slain.
sentences.s104-s42: Today I took account of the badness of my youth and the goodness of my days.
sentences.s104-s43: For those same rebels came up to me, each trailing a standard behind him.
sentences.s104-s44: They gave me many reminders of the vicissitudes of fate (of later times), and I fled, wretched and despised.
sentences.s104-s25: If such an encroachment had befallen Sam, you would not have remained triumphant over the Turks.
sentences.s104-s26: There is no counsel except to seek reconciliation, for with him your army has no footing.
sentences.s104-s27: Afridun fashioned a tract of the earth, and thereupon entrusted it to Tur the valiant.
sentences.s104-s28: They had given it to me and truly forgiven it; you ought not to seek vengeance for the past.
sentences.s104-s37: Where were the kings? Zal shattered (their) wisdom — he struck him with a heavy mace in a violent assault.
sentences.s104-s38: Shamasas, the hate‑bearing refuge of the host — whom Qaran killed on the battlefield.
sentences.s104-s39: Apart from these famed men, another hundred thousand more were slain on the field of battle.
sentences.s104-s40: Worse than all this was the fame and shame of defeat — a defeat that could never be made right.
